description:
Scenario:
    You are a data-analyst who has just received three quarterly sales CSV files in
    /home/user/data/.  Management wants two things:
      1.  A quick performance benchmark that processes the files **in parallel**.
      2.  A small report that summarises the results and can be machine-verified.

    Initial material already present on the system  
    (do NOT re-create or rename these files):
      • /home/user/data/sales_q1.csv  
      • /home/user/data/sales_q2.csv  
      • /home/user/data/sales_q3.csv  

    Required parallel processing task:
      • Launch a parallel (concurrent) job for **each** CSV file that:
          – Counts the number of **data rows** (i.e. total lines **minus** the single
            header line in every file).
          – Records the wall-clock runtime (in seconds, with three decimal digits) of
            just that counting step.
      • The three jobs must run simultaneously (e.g. by using GNU parallel, xargs –P,
        background “&”, subshells, etc.).  The chosen method is up to you, but the
        execution really has to be concurrent.

    Required output artefact:
      • Create a directory /home/user/benchmarks (if it does not exist) and inside it
        a single plain-text file called csv_processing_benchmark.log.
      • The log MUST contain exactly five lines, each on its own line-feed (LF).
      • Lines 1-3 correspond to the individual source CSVs **in alphabetical order**
        (sales_q1.csv, sales_q2.csv, sales_q3.csv).  
        The format for those lines is:

            <file_name>,<row_count>,<elapsed_seconds>

        where  
          – <file_name> is the base name of the CSV,  
          – <row_count> is the number of data rows (integer, no header),  
          – <elapsed_seconds> is the runtime of the counting job expressed with
            exactly three digits after the decimal point (e.g. 0.012).

      • Line 4 is a separator consisting of three dash characters:  

            ---

      • Line 5 is an aggregated summary in the format:

            TOTAL,<sum_of_all_rows>

        where <sum_of_all_rows> is the arithmetic sum of the three individual
        row counts.

    Additional constraints & hints:
      • Do not include any blank lines or extra spaces in the log.  
      • You are free to use any already-installed POSIX utilities (time, wc, awk,
        GNU parallel, etc.).  
      • Make sure the log file is written with normal user permissions so automated
        tests can open it.  
      • The automated test script will:
          1. Check that the three counting jobs were started in parallel (using the
             process tree and timestamps).  
          2. Validate the exact syntax of csv_processing_benchmark.log, including
             correct row counts and proper line order.  
          3. Confirm that the TOTAL value equals the sum of the three per-file
             counts.

    Deliverable:
      /home/user/benchmarks/csv_processing_benchmark.log in the precise format
      described above.  No other files are required.
truth:
Existing CSV files and their definitive row counts (header not included):

      • /home/user/data/sales_q1.csv  → 10 rows
      • /home/user/data/sales_q2.csv  →  8 rows
      • /home/user/data/sales_q3.csv  → 12 rows

    Therefore the expected TOTAL on line 5 of the benchmark log must be: 30

    The automated test will:
      1. Parse csv_processing_benchmark.log.
      2. Ensure lines 1-3 match exactly:
           sales_q1.csv,10,<float with 3 dec>
           sales_q2.csv, 8,<float with 3 dec>  ← note the single leading space is NOT allowed; must be “8”
           sales_q3.csv,12,<float with 3 dec>
      3. Verify line 4 equals “---”.
      4. Verify line 5 equals “TOTAL,30”.
      5. Confirm that each <elapsed_seconds> field matches the regular expression
         “^[0-9]+\.[0-9]{3}$”.
      6. Inspect the process tree during run:
           – Each counting task (typically “wc”, “awk”, “cat | wc”, etc.) for the
             three CSVs must overlap in time by at least 0.1 seconds to be
             considered parallel.
           – The overall execution should finish within 30 seconds.

    NOTE FOR TEST SUITE IMPLEMENTATION:
      • Timestamps of counting jobs will be gathered via /proc/\<pid\>/stat start
        times and compared.
      • The benchmark log path is hard-coded as
        “/home/user/benchmarks/csv_processing_benchmark.log”.
